name: The Holocaust (Shoah) and Nazi genocides
regions: Axis-aligned and collaborationist territories in Europe
summary: The Holocaust narrowly refers to the Nazi-led genocide of Europe’s Jews, in which about six million Jews were murdered. In this broader requested scope, it also includes Nazi and collaborator mass murder of Roma and Sinti, disabled people, non-Jewish Poles, Soviet POWs and civilians, Serb civilians under the Ustaša, gay men, Jehovah’s Witnesses, political opponents, and other targeted groups. Depending on definition, the total death toll ranges from about 11.2 million itemized victims to as high as about 17 million under broad Nazi racial-policy and occupation-policy definitions.

sides.name: Nazi Germany, Axis allies, SS/police, Wehrmacht, and collaborationist regimes
members: Wehrmacht units involved in occupation, POW, reprisal, and anti-partisan policies

military_killed.notes: Perpetrator military deaths cannot be cleanly separated for the Holocaust/Nazi genocides as distinct from wider World War II losses.

breakdown.notes: USHMM estimates approximately 2.7 million Jews murdered at killing centers.
USHMM estimates about 2 million Jews murdered in mass shooting operations and related massacres.
USHMM states at least 250,000 Jews were murdered in other acts of violence outside camps and ghettos; this category includes some deprivation and transport/forced-march deaths.
USHMM estimates Germans killed 1.8–1.9 million non-Jewish Polish civilians; direct and indirect components are not cleanly separated.
USHMM gives at least 250,000 and possibly 500,000 Roma/Sinti and others labeled 'Gypsies' murdered.

breakdown.victims_group: Disabled people in institutions and care facilities
breakdown.notes: USHMM estimates 250,000–300,000 disabled people murdered, including at least 10,000 children.

breakdown.victims_group: Serb civilians murdered by Ustaša authorities
breakdown.notes: USHMM lists more than 310,000 Serb civilians murdered by Ustaša authorities of the Independent State of Croatia.
USHMM lists about 1,700 Jehovah’s Witnesses killed in concentration camps or executed for refusing military service.

breakdown.victims_group: Gay men, bisexual men, and men accused of homosexuality
breakdown.notes: USHMM gives deaths as hundreds, possibly thousands, and states that 5,000–15,000 men were imprisoned in concentration camps as 'homosexual' offenders; the number who died is unknown.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The low/high subtotal is an approximate accounting of major directly killed or mixed direct-killing civilian categories. It should not be treated as a precise direct-killing total because several source categories combine shootings, camps, deprivation, deportation, disease, and forced labor.

deaths_from_actions.notes: USHMM estimates 800,000–1,000,000 Jews murdered in ghettos, concentration camps, and labor camps through deliberate privation, disease, brutal treatment, and arbitrary acts of violence.
USHMM estimates 3.3 million Soviet POWs died in German custody out of 5.7 million captured; methods included starvation, exposure, disease, shootings, and forced labor.

Alex J. Kay’s framework includes at least 2 million Soviet urban residents targeted by hunger policy; broader Snyder/Bloodlands-style summaries cite 4.2 million Soviet citizens starved by German occupiers. These figures risk overlap with POW and Jewish ghetto starvation categories if mechanically added.
Scholarly estimates vary; one source gives at least three-quarters of a million civilian deaths in Leningrad, while other summaries cite over one million deaths resulting from the siege.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Polish civilians and other forced laborers
deaths_from_actions.notes: At least 1.5 million Polish citizens were deported to German territory for forced labor, but deaths are not cleanly separated from the overall 1.8–1.9 million non-Jewish Polish civilian death estimate.

sides.name: Targeted peoples, prisoners, and resistance movements
members: Gay men, bisexual men, and men accused of homosexuality

military_killed.notes: This category counts Soviet POWs as captive combatants killed by Nazi policy, not as deaths caused by the targeted side. Resistance fighter deaths cannot be cleanly totaled separately from POW, civilian, and general World War II military losses.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Within Holocaust/Nazi-genocide accounting, the targeted groups were overwhelmingly victims, not perpetrators of systematic civilian mass killing.

totals.notes: Grand low is a conservative itemized roll-up of major USHMM/Yad Vashem-style categories; grand high reflects broad definitions including Soviet civilian starvation and occupation-policy deaths. Civilian high is grand high minus the 3.3 million Soviet POWs. Totals are not obtained by mechanically adding every subcomponent because Soviet starvation, camp, ghetto, and POW categories can overlap depending on definition.

key_atrocities.name: Auschwitz-Birkenau
key_atrocities.victims_group: Jews, Poles, Roma and Sinti, Soviet POWs, and others
key_atrocities.notes: Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum estimates around 1.1 million deaths: about 1 million Jews, 70,000 Poles, 21,000 Roma and Sinti, 15,000 Soviet POWs, and 12,000 others.

key_atrocities.name: Operation Reinhard killing centers
key_atrocities.notes: USHMM's listed Jewish killing-center figures include Chełmno at least 152,000, Bełżec about 435,000, Sobibór at least 167,000, and Treblinka II about 925,000. Auschwitz is separately listed in this JSON.

key_atrocities.name: Babyn Yar / Babi Yar massacre
key_atrocities.victims_group: Jewish civilians of Kyiv and vicinity
key_atrocities.notes: The first and best-documented massacre at Babyn Yar on September 29–30, 1941 killed 33,771 Jews.

key_atrocities.name: Warsaw Uprising suppression and Wola massacres
key_atrocities.perpetrator: German forces, SS, police, and auxiliaries
key_atrocities.notes: USHMM states the number of victims in the Warsaw Uprising exceeded 180,000; approximately 40,000 civilians were murdered in the Wola massacres between August 5 and 7, 1944.

key_atrocities.name: Nazi persecution and killing of Soviet POWs
key_atrocities.notes: USHMM estimates 3.3 million Soviet POWs died in German custody out of 5.7 million captured.

key_atrocities.name: Nazi euthanasia program / Aktion T4 and related killings
key_atrocities.victims_group: Disabled people in institutions and care facilities
key_atrocities.notes: USHMM estimates 250,000–300,000 disabled people murdered, including at least 10,000 children.

uncertainty_notes: The largest uncertainty is definitional: the narrow Shoah total is about six million Jews, while broader Nazi-genocide totals include Roma and Sinti, disabled people, Soviet POWs, Poles, Soviet civilians, Serbs, political and religious dissenters, gay men, and others. Direct versus indirect killing cannot be cleanly separated for many groups because camps, ghettos, forced labor, deportations, siege warfare, anti-partisan operations, starvation, disease, exposure, and executions were intertwined. Soviet civilian starvation figures can overlap with POW, Jewish ghetto, or siege categories if added mechanically.

sources.title: How Many People did the Nazis Murder?

sources.publisher: Holocaust and Genocide Studies / Oxford Academic

sources.title: Empire of destruction: A history of Nazi mass killing
